summary: McKenzie details his experience integrating Stripe as a payment processor for Bingo Card Creator, praising its API design, developer experience, and support quality. He then walks through a series of A/B tests comparing Stripe's on-site credit card checkout against PayPal/Google Checkout off-site options, finding that customers overwhelmingly prefer credit cards (90% vs 5% each for PayPal and Google Checkout). The breakthrough came not from checkout design alone but from reducing the free trial limit from 15 to 8 cards, which combined synergistically with the redesigned Stripe-powered checkout to produce massive revenue gains. Year-over-year sales increased 38-108% across several months. He credits the combination of good infrastructure (Stripe), professional design, and rigorous A/B testing for the results.
key_points: Stripe's API integration took only 29 lines of code and 3 hours to go live, versus weeks with most payment providers — making transaction fee comparisons irrelevant when integration costs dwarf the savings
When given the choice, 90% of customers chose credit card checkout over PayPal or Google Checkout, but simply adding credit cards didn't initially increase total conversions
Reducing the free trial limit from 15 to 8 cards produced a synergistic effect with the redesigned checkout, nearly doubling conversion rates (1.04% to 1.89%) with 99% statistical confidence
Stripe's co-founder personally debugged a dependency conflict using git bisect and shipped a fix for the ruby library — exemplifying developer-first support culture
The combination of infrastructure quality, professional design, and data-driven decision making compounded into year-over-year revenue increases of 33-108%
money_quotes: We sell products which have margins that come very close to 100%, and saving pennies on transactions to spend tens of thousands on integration costs or to shave full percentages off our conversion rates is absolute madness.
A/B testing prints money. So does having sufficient metrics in place such that one knows where the high priority places to A/B test are.
I am not easily emotionally moved by git command lines, but this is clearly somebody who understands me and what I need in life.
That email was signed by the co-founder. Patrick Collison, when he isn't running a payments company, apparently found time to verse himself in arcane git commands.
It's like they found the sweet spot between 'Computers can make decisions in lightning-speed at scale' and 'Humans can actually be trusted with discretion.'
This is really, really important for developers because you get end-to-end control of the user experience.

I occasionally write about companies which I use in my business and I feel are relevant to you guys. Stripe isn't a client. I haven't accepted anything of value from them… well, OK, technically speaking they have deposited $30,000 into my bank account, but you know what I mean.
So I tested the stupidest thing that could possibly work to get more people to hit the trial limitations: decrease people's free quota from 15 cards to 8 cards.
key_insight: Developer experience and end-to-end control of the checkout flow matter far more than transaction fees, because the revenue gains from better conversion optimization dwarf any savings on processing costs.
